content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Street, Suite 322
[illegible]
5 322-5650

July 22, 1975

Mr. Joseph T. Edmiston
Southern California Coastal Coordinator
Sierra Club
2410 Beverly Blvd., Ste. 2
Los Angeles, Calif. 90057

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-149

Dear Mr. Edmiston:

Thank you for your letter dated July 3, 1975,
requesting an opinion from the Fair Political Practices
Commission regarding "certain exchanges which may be in
connection with lobbying activities, but are primarily
personal in nature."

Your request for a formal opinion is denied
because the questions presented do not raise substantial
questions of interpretation under the Political Reform
Act.

Your opinion request poses six questions, to
which the Commission replies as follows:

(1) Under the facts presented, the cost of meals
eaten with your spouse is not reportable under the Political Reform Act.

(2) Your employer does not have to report the
cost of these meals as reimbursement.

(3) The cost of meals eaten with your spouse
would not be reportable if your spouse were a lobbyist,
unless paid for with funds from your lobbying account.
Disbursements from this account must be reported under
Government Code Section 86107, as amplified by 2 Cal.
Admin. Code Section 18615.

(4) If any of the persons listed in sections
(a) through (d) of your question are elective state officials, legislative officials, state candidates, agency

Mr. Joseph T. Edmiston
July 22, 1975
Page Two

officials of an agency before which you lobby, or members
of the immediate families of any such persons, you must
report the nature and date of any exchange, pursuant to
Government Code Section 86107(2). (See also 2 Cal. Admin.
Code Section 18650.)

Unreimbursed meals with social friends which are
not engaged in for the purpose of influencing legislation
or administrative action, are not reportable.

Reimbursement from your employer would affect the
result, because meals paid for cut a lobbyist account
must be reported pursuant to Section 86107 and the regula-
tions promulgated thereunder.

(5) The purchase of a meal as described is not
reportable unless reimbursed or carried out for the purpose
of influencing legislation or administrative action.

(6) If these expenditures are unreimbursed pay-
ments out of your salary, the employer has no reporting
obligations. However, you should note the provisions of
Section 86106, requiring that payments in connection with
lobbying activities must be made from a lobbying account.
If part of your salary is paid with the express or implied
understanding that you will make disbursements in support
of your lobbying activity, your employer should set up a
separate account to cover those expenses.

The Commission's draft opinion requested by
John C. Morrissey, Pacific Gas and Electric Company, No.
75-065-C, poses a series of situations where the lobbyist
is reimbursed for the meal.

Although for the reasons stated, no formal opinion
will be issued, I hope the foregoing is sufficiently help-
ful. If you have any questions, I urge you to contact a
member of the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the
Commission

DHL:jp

SIERRA CLUB
OFFICE OF THE SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
COASTAL COORDINATOR
2410 Beverly Boulevard, Suite 2
Los Angeles, California 90057
(213) 382-5793

Date Received 7/3/73
Response Due 7/3/75

July 3, 1975

Michael Bennett
Executive Director,
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
926 "J" Street, Suite 532
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: Request for Commission Opinion as
to Certain Exchanges which may be
"in connection with" Lobbying Act-
ivities, but are Primarily Personal
in Nature

Dear Mr. Bennett:

This letter is to request an opinion of the Commission, under Government
Code §83114, relative to certain activities which may incidentally touch
upon subjects within the scope of my employment as a lobbyist.

I. FACTUAL BACKGROUND

1. I am employed by the Sierra Club through funds donated by the Sierra
Club Foundation, and am registered as a lobbyist for purposes of influencing the California Coastal Zone Conservation Commission and several regional commissions created by Proposition 20 (Public Resources Code §27000 et
seq.). My wife, Nancy Parthian, is Executive Director of the Ecology Center
of Southern California. She is not registered as a lobbyist, although at
some time in the future she may become a lobbyist. By pre-nuptial agreement,
and several subsequent agreements, all income and assets owned or earned by
each of us are our separate property with only one minor exception.

2. The nature of my wife's employment is such that she deals daily in conservation and environmental matters, some of which are relative to activities
of the California Coastal Zone Conservation Commission, although they involve
public information and are not attempts to influence the actions of that
agency.

3. As could be expected, my wife and I frequently discuss current conservation matters, including frequently the activities of the Coastal Commissions.
We do this at dinners at home and very frequently at meals in restaurants.
None of these meals is reimbursed by my employer, nor are they reimbursed to her by
her employer. The information gained in such conversations is sometimes used
in my activity before the Coastal Commission, although the primary purpose of
such discussion is not related to lobbying activity but rather normal social

MICHAEL BENNETT
July 3, 1975                                                    Page Two


conversation.

4. Separate from the above activities, I frequently eat meals with persons
who are not lobbyists, but do appear before the Coastal Commission. Sometimes
I purchase meals for them, buy drinks, etc. None of this is reimbursed by my
employer. During such meals the conversation frequently touches upon the work
of the Coastal Commission and mutual concerns of the conservation community
with the activities of that agency.

5. On occasion I eat meals with other members of the Sierra Club staff,
volunteers who are attending meetings of the Coastal Commission and who are
reimbursed for actual expenses, and registered lobbyists for the Sierra Club.
Discussion of matters of general interest in the conservation field, and some-
times specific items that my touch upon the work of the Coastal Commissions,
are frequent at such meals.

                              II. QUESTIONS

1. Are the cost of meals eaten with Ms. Peariman at which conservation mat-
ters within the scope of activities of the Coastal Commissions are discussed
reportable under the Political Reform Act of 1974; notwithstanding the fact
that they are paid for out of salary and not reimbursements for lobbying
activities?

2. If the answer to question 1 is yes; then must my employers report that
portion of salary going to pay for the meals in question as payments by them
for the purpose of lobbying activity?                              wife

3. If the answer to question 1 is no; would the situation change if my/ours
became a registered lobbyist dealing generally in conservation activities
but not specifically registered to lobby the Coastal Commissions?

4. Are the cost of meals eaten with (a) Sierra Club staff members, (b) Sierra
Club lobbyists, (c) non-lobbyists but persons appearing before the Coastal
Commissions, (d) lobbyists employed by other groups to appear before the
Coastal Commissions, reportable under the Act if matters relating to the
California Coastal Zone Conservation Commissions are discussed? Would the
fact that no reimbursement from my employers is made for these meals affect
the result?

5. Is the purchase of a meal for someone else, not an agency official or
legislator, at which matters concerning the Coastal Commissions are discussed
reportable under the Act?

6. If the answer to questions 4 and 5 is yes; must my employers report such
sums as payments for the purpose of lobbying activity?

MICHAEL BENNETT
July 3, 1975                                                     Page Three


                    III.  DISCUSSION

Ordinarily one would not think that normal conversation with one's wife or
friends about the nature of my work or the problems encountered therein,
would mean that any cost associated with such conversations, e.g., the cost
of the meal, drinks, etc., would have to be reported as a gift or employer
as expenses "in connection with [my] activities as a lobbyist." Government
Code §86106(a). However, the draft opinion in the Matter of John C. Morrissey,
No. 75-005-C, raises the question in a new light. In that opinion staff, the
staff says "the phrase 'in connection with' lobbyist activities is a broad
one, and that the meal should be reported if it in any respect is intended
to further the lobbyist's activities. It need not be reported only if it is
completely unrelated to lobbying activities." (pp. 4-5 [emphasis added])

It is my contention, that in primarily social conversations, no valid purpose
of the act is to be served by requiring reporting of the cost of meals with
friends, relatives, and associates which are not reimbursed by my employer as
lobbying activity expenses, but rather are paid from my salary alone. This is
particularly true since the conduct of most of these discussions relates to
general conservation matters, of which some parts of the subject matter thereof
may come before the Coastal Commissions or be part of the Coastal Zone Con-
servation Plan which is being prepared by said commissions, but the primary
purpose of which is to engage in social chitchat as opposed to business negotiation.
The language of the draft opinion, however, seems to require a reporting of
each and every conversation that may have a cost associated with it "if it in
any respect affects a lobbyist's activities, or is not "completely unrelated"
to such activities.

In the case of conversations with my wife, who is also professionally involved
in conservation, it means that those meals paid for by her at which we discuss
conservation need not be reported (since we have a separate property agreement),
but those which I pay for must be reported, at least on a pro-rata basis, if
the Commission strictly follows the language in the Morrissey opinion. This
would lead to burdensome reporting and accounting by myself without substantial
benefit to the public in terms of actual amounts spent to influence agency
action. The rule of reason should be applied in these cases to cover only
those meals the primary purpose of which is to influence agency action, and
to totally exclude normal marital conversations with my wife. If this limitation
is not made, the effect will be an intervention in my private life unwarranted
by any of the legitimate objectives of the Fair Political Practices Act, and
which may unconstitutionally violate the right of marital privacy.


                                            Very truly yours,


                                            JOSEPH T. ENNERSON
                                            Southern California
                                            Coastal Coordinator

sections.question:
1. Are the cost of meals eaten with Ms. Pearman at which conservation matters within the scope of activity of the Coastal Commissions are discussed reportable under the Political Reform Act of 1974; notwithstanding the fact that they are paid for out of salary and not reimbursements for lobbying activities?

2. If the answer to question 1 is yes; then must my employers report that portion of salary going to pay for the meals in question as payments by them for the purpose of lobbying activity?

wife

3. If the answer to question 1 is no; would the situation change if my wife were to become a registered lobbyist dealing generally in conservation activities but not specifically registered to lobby the Coastal Commissions?

4. Are the cost of meals eaten with (a) Sierra Club staff members, (b) Sierra Club lobbyists, (c) non-lobbyists but persons appearing before the Coastal Commissions, (d) lobbyists employed by other groups to appear before the Coastal Commissions, reportable under the Act if matters relating to the California Coastal Zone Conservation Commissions are discussed? Would the fact that no reimbursement from my employers is made for these meals affect the result?

5. Is the purchase of a meal for someone else, not an agency official or legislator, at which matters concerning the Coastal Commissions are discussed reportable under the Act?

6. If the answer to questions 4 and 5 is yes; must my employers report such sums as payments for the purpose of lobbying activity?
sections.analysis:
Ordinarily one would not think that normal conversation with one's wife or friends about the nature of my work or the problems encountered therein, would mean that any cost associated with such conversations, e.g., the cost of the meal, drinks, etc., would have to be reported by myself or my employer as expenses "in connection with [my] activities as a lobbyist." Government Code §36106(a). However, the draft opinion in the Matter of John C. Morrissey, No. 75-065-C, casts the question in a new light. In that opinion draft, the staff says "the phrase 'in connection with' lobbying activities is a broad one, and that the meal should be reported if it in any respect is intended to further the lobbyist's activities. It need not be reported only if it is completely unrelated to lobbying activities." (pp. 4-5 [emphasis added])

It is my contention, that in primarily social conversations, no valid purpose of the act is to be served by requiring reporting of the cost of meals with friends, relatives, and associates which are not reimbursed by my employer as lobbying activity expenses, but rather are paid from my salary alone. This is particularly true since the content of most of these discussions relates to general conservation matters, of which some parts of the subject matter thereof may come before the Coastal Commissions or be part of the Coastal Zone Conservation Plan which is being prepared by said commissions, but the primary purpose of which is to engage in social as opposed to business communication. The language of the draft opinion, however, seems to require a reporting of each and every conversation that may have a cost associated with it "if it in any respect" affects a lobbyist's activities, or is not "completely unrelated" to such activities.

In the case of conversations with my wife, who is also professionally involved in conservation, it means that those meals paid for by her at which we discuss conservation need not be reported (since we have a separate property agreement), but those which I pay for must be reported, at least on a pro-rata basis, if the Commission strictly follows the language in the Morrissey opinion. This would lead to burdensome reporting and accounting by myself without substantial benefit to the public in terms of actual amounts spent to influence agency action. The rule of reason should be applied in these cases to cover only those meals the primary purpose of which is to influence agency action, and to totally exclude normal marital conversations with my wife. If this limitation is not made, the effect will be an intervention in my private life unwarranted by any of the legitimate objectives of the Fair Political Practices Act, and which may unconstitutionally violate the right of marital privacy.
